---
title: "Group analysis"
subtitle: "Second-level inference over first-level estimates, fully typed."
jupyter: python3
execute:
  freeze: auto
---

This is the last stage of the workflow. A first-level model gives you
one effect estimate per subject; a *second-level* (group) analysis asks
whether that effect holds across subjects, and how it varies with
subject-level variables. Per-subject estimates flow into a `GroupData`
container and out through result objects you read by attribute. The
group design is written as a formula, the same way you would write it in
R or statsmodels.

Three functions in `fmrimod.stats` cover this stage. They are the same
underlying model seen from different angles, so pick by what you want
back:

- **`fmri_meta`** — the workhorse. Meta-analysis when the formula is
  `~ 1`; meta-regression when you add a covariate (`~ 1 + C(group)`).
  Returns an `FmriMetaResult`.
- **`fmri_ttest`** — a thin shortcut for the plain one-sample
  *t*-test, when that is literally all you want. Returns an
  `FmriTTestResult`.
- **`group_fit`** — one canonical door that *always* returns the same
  `GroupFitResult`, whatever model you ask for. Worth it when many fits
  must come back as one uniform type (a sweep, a pipeline stage).

If you only read one section, read `fmri_meta` below — it is enough for
almost every analysis.

## Per-Subject Estimates → Typed `GroupData`

A second-level analysis starts from one effect (and its standard error) per
subject per feature. Stack them as a tidy frame and let
`group_data_from_csv` build the typed container — it accepts an in-memory
DataFrame and a covariate table, so the group design is declared by data,
not assembled by hand.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import fmrimod as fm
from fmrimod.dataset import group_data_from_csv
from fmrimod.stats import fmri_meta, group_fit, GroupFitRequest, fmri_ttest

rng = np.random.default_rng(12)
n_subj, n_feat = 10, 6
subjects = [f"s{i + 1}" for i in range(n_subj)]
group = np.repeat(["ctrl", "patient"], n_subj // 2)

# Synthetic first-level betas + sampling variances (the fixture only).
true = np.array([[0.2] * n_feat, [0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.0, -0.1, -0.2]])
V = rng.uniform(0.04, 0.12, size=(n_subj, n_feat))
gnum = (group == "patient").astype(int)
Y = np.column_stack([np.ones(n_subj), gnum]) @ true + rng.normal(scale=np.sqrt(V))

long = pd.DataFrame([
    {"subject": s, "roi": f"feature_{j + 1}",
     "beta": Y[i, j], "se": np.sqrt(V[i, j])}
    for i, s in enumerate(subjects)
    for j in range(n_feat)
])

gd = group_data_from_csv(
    long,
    effect_cols={"beta": "beta", "se": "se"},
    subject_col="subject",
    roi_col="roi",
    covariates=pd.DataFrame({"group": group}),
)
gd.format, len(gd.subjects)
```

## One-Sample Random Effects

The common second-level question — "is the effect non-zero across subjects?"
— is a random-effects meta-analysis with an intercept-only formula.
`fmri_meta` returns a typed `FmriMetaResult`: read `.coefficients`, `.p`,
`.tau2` as attributes.

```{python}
one = fmri_meta(gd, formula="~ 1", method="pm")

pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": one.feature_names,
    "estimate": one.coefficients[:, 0],
    "p": one.p[:, 0],
    "tau2": one.tau2,
}).round(3)
```

## One Result Type for Every Fit: `group_fit`

`fmri_meta` and `fmri_ttest` each return their own result type. When you
run many second-level models and want them all to come back as the
*same* object, `group_fit` is the single entry that always returns a
`GroupFitResult`.

Called on a dataset alone, it runs **one explicit default**: a
one-sample (`formula="~ 1"`) **random-effects meta-analysis**, with
Paule–Mandel `tau2` and inverse-variance weights. That is the same model
as `fmri_meta(gd, formula="~ 1", method="pm")` — `group_fit(gd)` is just
the uniform-result-type door to it.

```{python}
res = group_fit(gd)
type(res).__name__, res.method, res.backend, res.estimate[:, 0].round(3)
```

For anything other than that default, pass a `GroupFitRequest`: a frozen
description of the *whole* fit — formula, model (`meta`/`ttest`), fixed
vs random effects, `tau2` method, weights, backend — as one value you
can build, inspect, and reuse instead of tracking many keyword
arguments. The request below just writes the default out in full so you
can see every knob it sets:

```{python}
res2 = group_fit(
    GroupFitRequest(
        data=gd,
        formula="~ 1",
        model="meta",
        effects="random",
        method="pm",
    )
)
res2.method, res2.estimate[:, 0].round(3)
```

For everyday analysis, call `fmri_meta`/`fmri_ttest` directly; reach for
`group_fit` when a uniform `GroupFitResult` across many fits is worth
more than the shorter call.

## Between-Group Meta-Regression

To test a group difference, keep the same `fmri_meta` call and just add
the covariate to the `formula`. With `method="dl"` this is a
*random-effects* meta-regression: it models between-subject
heterogeneity (`tau2`) while estimating the group effect. The
`C(group)[T.patient]` coefficient is the patient-minus-control contrast
per feature.

```{python}
reg = fmri_meta(gd, formula="~ 1 + C(group)", method="dl")

pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": reg.feature_names,
    "patient_minus_ctrl": reg.coefficients[:, 1],
    "p": reg.p[:, 1],
}).round(3)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Choosing `method`

`method` controls how between-subject heterogeneity is handled. Which
values are valid depends on whether the formula has a covariate.

**One-sample (`formula="~ 1"`):**

- `"pm"` (Paule–Mandel), `"reml"` — random effects, iterative `tau2`;
  the usual choices for a one-sample test.
- `"dl"` (DerSimonian–Laird) — random effects, closed-form `tau2`.
- `"fe"` — fixed effects: assumes a single true effect with no
  between-subject variance.

**Covariate formula / meta-regression (`formula="~ 1 + …"`):**

- `"dl"` — random-effects meta-regression: models `tau2` *and* the
  covariate. The usual choice, and the one used above.
- `"fe"` — fixed-effects meta-regression: the covariate effect with no
  between-subject variance.
- `"pm"` and `"reml"` are intercept-only; passing one a covariate
  formula raises `NotImplementedError` rather than silently fitting a
  different model.

Rule of thumb: `pm`/`reml` for a one-sample random-effects test, `dl`
once the design has a covariate (or `fe` when you specifically want a
fixed-effects model).
:::

## One-Sample Test, One Call

For a plain one-sample second-level *t*-test, `fmri_ttest` is the typed
shortcut — same `GroupData`, a `FmriTTestResult` out.

```{python}
tt = fmri_ttest(gd, formula="~ 1")
type(tt).__name__, tt.engine, tt.statistic.round(2)
```

## Next

- [Functional connectivity](functional-connectivity.qmd)
- [Benchmark datasets](benchmark-datasets.qmd)
